_note: Step-by-step iTax filing instructions for Kenyan SMEs. Source: KRA iTax portal procedures. Updated 2026-03-29.

filing_guides.title: How to File Turnover Tax (TOT) on iTax
steps: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke using your KRA PIN and password.
On the dashboard, click 'Returns' in the top menu bar.
Select 'File Returns' from the dropdown.
In the 'Tax Obligation' dropdown, select 'Turnover Tax'.
Select the return period (month and year) you are filing for.
Enter your total gross turnover for the month — this is ALL revenue before any expenses or deductions.
The system will automatically calculate 3% of your turnover as the tax due.
Review the computed amount carefully. Confirm it matches your records.
If you have already made any advance payments, enter them in the 'Payments' section.
Click 'Submit' to file the return.
You will receive an acknowledgement receipt. Download and save it.
If tax is due, proceed to 'Payment Registration' to generate a payment slip.
Pay via M-Pesa (Paybill 572572, Account = your Payment Registration Number), bank, or KRA e-payment portal.
Keep the M-Pesa confirmation message or bank slip as proof of payment.
documents_needed: KRA PIN certificate
iTax login credentials (PIN + password)
Monthly sales records or summary (total gross turnover for the month)
M-Pesa or bank statement showing business receipts for cross-checking
Previous month's TOT return (for reference)
common_mistakes: Confusing gross turnover with net profit — TOT is on total revenue, not profit.
Forgetting to include cash sales in the turnover figure.
Filing for the wrong period (e.g. filing January instead of February).
Not filing a NIL return when there was no income — you must still file even if turnover is zero.
Missing the 20th deadline and getting hit with the KES 20,000 late filing penalty.
Businesses with turnover above KES 25M or below KES 1M are not eligible for TOT.
tips: Set a calendar reminder for the 15th of each month so you have time to prepare before the 20th deadline.
Keep a simple daily sales log — it makes monthly filing much easier.
If your business turnover grows beyond KES 25M annually, you must switch to regular income tax. Plan ahead.
TOT replaces income tax, so you do NOT file a separate income tax return for this business income.
You can file a NIL return on iTax if you had zero turnover for the month.

filing_guides.title: How to File VAT Returns on iTax
steps: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke using your KRA PIN and password.
Click 'Returns' in the top menu, then select 'File Returns'.
Select 'VAT' from the 'Tax Obligation' dropdown.
Select the return period (month and year).
You will see the VAT return form (VAT3). It has multiple sections.
Section A — Sales/Output VAT: Enter all your sales for the month. Categorise them as Standard Rated (16%), Zero Rated (0%), or Exempt.
For standard rated sales, enter the total taxable value. The system calculates output VAT at 16%.
Section B — Purchases/Input VAT: Enter all your business purchases. Categorise them similarly.
For purchases with VAT, enter the total taxable value. The system calculates input VAT you can claim.
Important: Only claim input VAT on purchases supported by valid eTIMS invoices or tax invoices.
Section C — The system computes VAT payable (Output VAT minus Input VAT). If positive, you owe KRA. If negative, you have a credit.
Review the summary carefully. Check that totals match your books.
Upload the CSV file if you have many line items (download the template from iTax first).
Click 'Submit' to file.
Download the acknowledgement receipt.
If VAT is payable, generate a Payment Registration slip and pay via M-Pesa (Paybill 572572), bank, or e-payment.
documents_needed: KRA PIN certificate
iTax login credentials
eTIMS-generated sales invoices for the month (all of them)
Purchase invoices with VAT (tax invoices or eTIMS invoices from your suppliers)
Sales summary categorised by: Standard Rated (16%), Zero Rated, Exempt
Purchases summary categorised by: Standard Rated, Zero Rated, Exempt, Out of Scope
Previous month's VAT return for reference
Import entry documents (if any imports were made)
Credit/debit notes issued or received during the month
common_mistakes: Claiming input VAT on purchases without valid tax invoices — KRA will disallow these.
Not issuing eTIMS invoices for all sales — mandatory from 2024 for all VAT-registered businesses.
Mixing up zero-rated and exempt supplies — they are treated differently for input VAT claims.
Forgetting to include imported goods and the VAT paid at customs.
Claiming input VAT on non-deductible items (e.g. entertainment, personal expenses).
Filing a NIL return when there were actually transactions — this triggers audits.
Not reversing input VAT on bad debts older than 2 years.
tips: Use the iTax CSV upload template for filing if you have many transactions — it saves time and reduces errors.
Reconcile your eTIMS records with your accounting system BEFORE filing.
Keep all purchase invoices for at least 5 years — KRA can audit that far back.
If you consistently have excess input VAT (refund position), apply for a VAT refund through iTax.
Zero-rated businesses can claim full input VAT refunds. Exempt businesses cannot claim input VAT at all.
Register for eTIMS at https://etims.kra.go.ke if you have not already — it is mandatory.

filing_guides.title: How to File PAYE Returns on iTax
steps: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke using your employer KRA PIN and password.
Click 'Returns' in the top menu, then select 'File Returns'.
Select 'PAYE' from the 'Tax Obligation' dropdown.
Select the return period (month and year you are filing for).
Download the PAYE CSV template from iTax (P10 return template).
Fill in the CSV with each employee's details: KRA PIN, name, gross pay, taxable pay, PAYE deducted, personal relief applied.
Calculate PAYE for each employee using the graduated tax bands: 10% (up to KES 24,000), 25% (KES 24,001-32,333), 30% (KES 32,334-500,000), 32.5% (KES 500,001-800,000), 35% (above KES 800,000).
Deduct the monthly personal relief of KES 2,400 per employee (if applicable).
Also deduct any insurance relief, disability exemption, or pension contribution relief for qualifying employees.
Upload the completed CSV file to iTax.
The system will validate the file. Fix any errors flagged (common: invalid PIN, wrong format).
Review the summary: total gross pay, total PAYE, total employees.
Click 'Submit' to file the return.
Download the acknowledgement receipt.
Generate a Payment Registration slip for the total PAYE amount.
Pay the total PAYE to KRA by the 9th of the following month via M-Pesa (Paybill 572572), bank, or e-payment.
documents_needed: KRA employer PIN and iTax credentials
Employee KRA PINs (every employee must have one)
Monthly payroll register showing: basic salary, allowances, benefits, gross pay, deductions, net pay for each employee
NSSF contribution details per employee
SHIF contribution details per employee
Housing Levy deduction details per employee
Pension/provident fund contribution details (if applicable)
Insurance policy details (for insurance relief calculations)
P10 CSV template downloaded from iTax
common_mistakes: Employees without KRA PINs — every employee must be registered. Help them register at https://itax.kra.go.ke.
Not deducting PAYE from casual workers earning above KES 2,000 per day.
Forgetting to apply personal relief (KES 2,400/month) — this reduces the employee's tax burden.
Using the wrong tax bands (they change with Finance Acts — verify current rates).
Including tax-exempt allowances (like per diem up to KES 2,000) in taxable income.
Filing after the 9th — the penalty is 25% of the tax due, which is severe.
Not including benefits-in-kind (e.g. company car, housing) in taxable pay.
tips: Invest in payroll software that auto-generates the P10 CSV. Manual calculation with many employees is error-prone.
The 9th deadline is earlier than most other taxes (which are the 20th). Mark it clearly in your calendar.
If you hire new employees mid-month, prorate their salary and PAYE for that month.
File NSSF, SHIF, and Housing Levy at the same time as PAYE — they all share the 9th deadline.
Keep copies of all payslips signed by employees. KRA may request them during audits.
If you have zero employees in a month, file a NIL PAYE return to avoid penalties.

filing_guides.title: How to File Individual Income Tax Returns on iTax
filing_guides.itax_menu_path: Returns > File Returns > Income Tax - Resident Individual
steps: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke using your personal KRA PIN and password.
Click 'Returns' in the top menu, then select 'File Returns'.
Select 'Income Tax - Resident Individual' from the 'Tax Obligation' dropdown.
Select the year of income you are filing for (e.g. 2025 income, filed by 30 June 2026).
The return form has several sections. Fill them in order.
Section 1 — Employment Income: Enter details from your P9A form (provided by your employer). Include gross pay, PAYE deducted, and employer details.
Section 2 — Business Income: If you run a sole proprietorship, enter gross income, allowable expenses, and net profit/loss.
Section 3 — Rental Income: Enter any rental income (unless you are filing MRI separately).
Section 4 — Other Income: Investment income (dividends, interest), capital gains, foreign income, etc.
Section 5 — Deductions and Reliefs: Enter pension contributions, insurance premiums, mortgage interest, disability exemption, and any other reliefs you qualify for.
The system calculates your total income, total tax liability, and credits for tax already paid (PAYE, withholding tax, advance tax).
Review the tax computation carefully. The balance is either tax payable or a refund due.
Attach supporting documents if required (P9A, financial statements, withholding tax certificates).
Click 'Submit' to file.
Download the acknowledgement receipt and the tax computation.
If tax is payable, generate a payment slip and pay by 30 June.
documents_needed: KRA PIN and iTax credentials
P9A tax deduction card from your employer(s) for the year
Business financial statements (profit and loss, balance sheet) if self-employed
Rental income records and expenses (if applicable)
Bank statements showing interest earned
Dividend vouchers or certificates
Withholding tax certificates (for any WHT deducted on your income)
Insurance premium receipts (for insurance relief)
Pension/provident fund contribution statements
Mortgage interest statement from your bank (for owner-occupied home relief)
Disability exemption certificate (if applicable)
common_mistakes: Not filing at all because 'my employer already deducted PAYE' — you must still file an annual return.
Forgetting to include all sources of income (freelance work, side business, rental, interest).
Not claiming reliefs you are entitled to: personal relief, insurance relief, pension relief.
Using the wrong year of income (remember: 2025 income is filed in 2026).
Not requesting your P9A form from your employer early enough.
Claiming expenses that are not allowable (personal expenses, capital expenditure without proper allowances).
Missing the 30 June deadline — penalty is 5% of tax due or KES 20,000, whichever is higher.
tips: Request your P9A from your employer by April so you have time to prepare.
If you have multiple income sources, keep them well-organised throughout the year.
Insurance relief is 15% of premiums paid, up to KES 60,000 per year.
Pension relief is 15% of contributions, up to KES 240,000 per year.
If you overpaid tax (refund due), apply for a refund through iTax after filing.
You can amend a return within 12 months of filing if you made a mistake.
Even with zero income, file a NIL return to stay compliant.

filing_guides.title: How to File Corporate Income Tax Returns on iTax
filing_guides.itax_menu_path: Returns > File Returns > Income Tax - Company
steps: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke using your company's KRA PIN and password.
Click 'Returns' in the top menu, then select 'File Returns'.
Select 'Income Tax - Company' from the 'Tax Obligation' dropdown.
Select the accounting period you are filing for.
Section 1 — Company Details: Verify company name, registration number, accounting period, and principal activity.
Section 2 — Income Statement: Enter gross turnover/revenue, cost of sales, and all operating expenses as per your audited accounts.
Section 3 — Tax Computation: Adjust accounting profit for tax purposes. Add back non-deductible expenses (entertainment, fines, donations above 10% of income, etc.).
Claim capital deductions (wear and tear, industrial building allowances, investment deductions) in the appropriate fields.
Section 4 — Tax Payable: The system applies the 30% corporate tax rate (or 25% for newly listed companies on NSE).
Enter any advance tax (instalment tax) already paid during the year (installments due 20 Apr, 20 Jun, 20 Sep, 20 Dec).
Enter any withholding tax credits (from WHT certificates received during the year).
Section 5 — Directors and Shareholders: Enter details of directors and shareholders holding 5% or more.
Upload required attachments: audited financial statements, tax computation, capital deduction schedules.
Review everything carefully, then click 'Submit'.
Download the acknowledgement receipt.
Pay any balance of tax due by the filing deadline.
documents_needed: Company KRA PIN and iTax credentials
Audited financial statements (mandatory for limited companies)
Tax computation prepared by your accountant or tax advisor
Capital deduction schedule (wear and tear allowances, etc.)
Withholding tax certificates received during the year
Instalment tax payment receipts (4 quarterly payments)
Schedule of directors and shareholders with 5%+ holding
Related party transaction details (if any)
Certificate of incorporation / CR12
Transfer pricing documentation (if the company has related-party cross-border transactions)
common_mistakes: Not paying instalment tax (advance tax) during the year — this triggers penalties even before the return is due.
Claiming non-deductible expenses (political donations, personal expenses of directors, fines).
Incorrect capital allowance calculations — using wrong rates or claiming on non-qualifying assets.
Not filing within 6 months of the accounting period end.
Forgetting to include all income — rental income from company property, interest, foreign income.
Not attaching audited accounts — the return may be rejected.
Failing to disclose related party transactions — this is a red flag for KRA.
tips: Engage a qualified tax advisor for corporate returns. The complexity justifies the cost.
Plan your instalment tax: each payment is 25% of your estimated annual tax. Underestimating by more than 10% triggers penalties.
Capital deduction rates vary by asset type: computers 30%, furniture 12.5%, motor vehicles 25%, buildings 10%. Use the correct rates.
Newly listed companies on the Nairobi Securities Exchange get a 25% rate for 5 years — claim this if applicable.
Keep all supporting documents for 7 years. KRA can audit corporate returns going back that far.
File early to avoid the last-minute rush and system downtime.

filing_guides.title: How to File Withholding Tax Returns on iTax
steps: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke using your KRA PIN and password.
Click 'Returns' in the top menu, then select 'File Returns'.
Select 'Withholding Tax' from the 'Tax Obligation' dropdown.
Select the return period (month and year).
Download the WHT CSV template from iTax.
For each qualifying payment you made during the month, fill in: payee's KRA PIN, payee name, nature of payment, gross amount, WHT rate applied, WHT amount deducted.
Common WHT rates: dividends (resident 5%), interest (15%), professional/management fees (5%), commercial rent (10%), contractual fees (3%).
Upload the completed CSV file to iTax.
The system validates the data. Fix any errors (e.g. invalid payee PIN, wrong rate).
Review the summary: total payments, total WHT deducted.
Click 'Submit' to file the return.
Download the acknowledgement receipt.
Generate WHT certificates for each payee — they need these to claim tax credits. Go to 'Certificates' > 'Withholding Tax Certificate' to generate.
Generate a payment slip and pay the total WHT to KRA by the 20th of the following month.
Pay via M-Pesa (Paybill 572572), bank, or e-payment.
documents_needed: KRA PIN and iTax credentials
List of all qualifying payments made during the month
Payee KRA PINs (you must have the PIN of everyone you deducted WHT from)
Invoices or contracts showing the nature and amount of each payment
WHT CSV template downloaded from iTax
Records of WHT rates applied to each payment type
common_mistakes: Not deducting WHT when required — if you pay professional fees, management fees, rent, etc., you must deduct and remit WHT.
Applying the wrong WHT rate for the payment type.
Not having the payee's KRA PIN — you cannot file without it. Get it before making payment.
Forgetting to issue WHT certificates to payees — they need these for their own tax returns.
Treating all payments to suppliers as subject to WHT — not all payments attract WHT, only specific types.
Not filing NIL when no qualifying payments were made in a month.
tips: Always ask for a supplier's KRA PIN before making the first payment. Add it to your supplier master file.
Create a simple tracker for all WHT payments: date, payee, amount, rate, WHT deducted.
WHT on rent: if you pay rent for your business premises, you must deduct 10% WHT and remit to KRA.
WHT on professional fees: applies to payments for legal, accounting, consulting, and other professional services.
Issue WHT certificates promptly. Your suppliers need them, and it builds good business relationships.
Non-resident payments may attract different (usually higher) WHT rates. Check the Double Taxation Agreement if applicable.

filing_guides.title: How to File NSSF Contributions
filing_guides.itax_menu_path: Filed via NSSF portal at https://eservices.nssf.or.ke (not iTax)
steps: Log in to the NSSF self-service portal at https://eservices.nssf.or.ke using your employer credentials.
Click on 'Contribution' then 'Upload Contribution File'.
Download the NSSF contribution template (Excel format).
Fill in each employee's details: NSSF number, national ID, name, gross earnings, employee contribution, employer contribution.
Calculate contributions: 6% of pensionable earnings, split equally between employer and employee. Tier I is on earnings up to KES 7,000. Tier II is on earnings between KES 7,001 and KES 36,000.
Example: an employee earning KES 30,000 — Tier I contribution = KES 420 each (6% of 7,000), Tier II contribution = KES 1,380 each (6% of 23,000). Total employee contribution = KES 1,800.
Upload the completed template to the portal.
The system validates the data. Fix any errors (missing NSSF numbers, calculation mismatches).
Confirm the total contribution amount (employer + employee).
Generate the payment advice.
Pay via M-Pesa (NSSF Paybill) or bank transfer by the 9th of the following month.
Download the payment receipt and contribution schedule for your records.
documents_needed: NSSF employer registration number
NSSF portal login credentials
Employee NSSF member numbers (ensure all employees are registered)
Monthly payroll showing gross earnings per employee
NSSF contribution template (downloaded from portal)
Employee national ID numbers
common_mistakes: Using the old NSSF rates (KES 200 flat per employee) — the new rates are 6% of pensionable pay in two tiers.
Not registering new employees with NSSF before filing — each employee must have an NSSF number.
Calculating contributions on basic salary only instead of total pensionable earnings.
Missing the 9th deadline — late contributions attract penalties.
Not including both Tier I and Tier II contributions.
Confusing the NSSF portal with iTax — NSSF has its own separate portal.
tips: Register new employees with NSSF immediately when they join. You can register online at the NSSF portal.
NSSF contributions are tax-deductible for the employer — factor this into your tax planning.
Employee NSSF contributions reduce taxable income for PAYE — ensure your payroll accounts for this.
File NSSF at the same time as PAYE, SHIF, and Housing Levy — they all share the 9th deadline.
Keep a record of all NSSF payments. Employees may request contribution statements when approaching retirement.
If you have no employees in a given month, you may still need to file a NIL return depending on your registration status.

filing_guides.title: How to File SHIF (Social Health Insurance Fund) Contributions
filing_guides.itax_menu_path: Filed via SHA portal at https://sha.go.ke (replaced NHIF)
steps: Log in to the Social Health Authority (SHA) portal at https://sha.go.ke using your employer credentials.
Navigate to the employer contribution filing section.
Download the SHIF contribution template.
Fill in each employee's details: national ID, SHA/SHIF number, name, gross salary, SHIF contribution amount.
Calculate SHIF: 2.75% of each employee's gross salary. This is deducted from the employee's pay.
Example: employee earning KES 50,000 gross — SHIF contribution = KES 1,375 (2.75% of 50,000).
Upload the completed template.
The system validates employee records. Fix any errors (unregistered employees, calculation mismatches).
Confirm the total contribution amount for all employees.
Generate the payment advice.
Pay via M-Pesa or bank transfer by the 9th of the following month.
Download the payment receipt for your records.
documents_needed: SHA employer registration details
SHA portal login credentials
Employee national ID numbers and SHA/SHIF member numbers
Monthly payroll showing gross salary per employee
SHIF contribution template (downloaded from SHA portal)
common_mistakes: Using old NHIF fixed rates instead of the new 2.75% of gross salary under SHIF.
Not registering employees on the SHA portal — each employee needs a SHIF number.
Calculating SHIF on basic salary instead of gross salary.
Missing the 9th deadline — same deadline as PAYE, NSSF, and Housing Levy.
Confusing SHA portal with the old NHIF portal — NHIF has been replaced by SHIF under SHA.
Not deducting SHIF for employees on probation — they are still eligible.
tips: SHIF replaced NHIF from October 2024. Make sure you are using the new rates and portal.
2.75% is on gross salary with no cap currently specified — verify current regulations as this may change.
Help employees register on the SHA portal if they have not migrated from NHIF.
File together with PAYE, NSSF, and Housing Levy to streamline your monthly payroll compliance.
Keep records of all SHIF remittances. Employees may need proof of contribution for healthcare access.
SHIF is an employee-only deduction (employer does not match), unlike NSSF and Housing Levy.

filing_guides.title: How to File Affordable Housing Levy on iTax
filing_guides.itax_menu_path: Returns > File Returns > Affordable Housing Levy
steps: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke using your employer KRA PIN and password.
Click 'Returns' in the top menu, then select 'File Returns'.
Select 'Affordable Housing Levy' from the 'Tax Obligation' dropdown.
Select the return period (month and year).
Download the Housing Levy CSV template from iTax.
Fill in each employee's details: KRA PIN, national ID, name, gross salary, employee levy (1.5%), employer levy (1.5%).
Calculate: Employee contributes 1.5% of gross salary. Employer matches with another 1.5% of the employee's gross salary.
Example: employee earning KES 50,000 — employee levy = KES 750, employer levy = KES 750, total = KES 1,500.
Upload the completed CSV to iTax.
The system validates the data. Fix any flagged errors.
Review the summary: total employee levy, total employer levy, grand total.
Click 'Submit' to file the return.
Download the acknowledgement receipt.
Generate a payment slip and pay the total amount (employee + employer portions) by the 9th of the following month.
Pay via M-Pesa (Paybill 572572), bank, or e-payment.
documents_needed: KRA employer PIN and iTax credentials
Employee KRA PINs and national ID numbers
Monthly payroll showing gross salary per employee
Housing Levy CSV template downloaded from iTax
Previous month's Housing Levy return for reference
common_mistakes: Forgetting the employer's matching 1.5% — the employer must also contribute, not just deduct from employees.
Calculating on basic salary instead of gross salary.
Not including all employees — all workers on payroll are subject to the levy.
Confusing the Housing Levy with NSSF or SHIF — they are separate obligations with separate filings.
Missing the 9th deadline — same deadline as PAYE.
Not filing when you have no employees — check if a NIL return is required based on your registration.
tips: The Housing Levy is filed on iTax (unlike NSSF and SHIF which have separate portals).
The employer's 1.5% contribution is an additional cost to the business — budget for it.
Housing Levy is not tax-deductible for the employee but the employer portion is a deductible business expense.
File at the same time as PAYE since they share the same deadline and use iTax.
Maintain a separate column in your payroll for Housing Levy to keep it clear.
This levy applies to all employees regardless of nationality or contract type.

filing_guides.title: How to File Monthly Rental Income (MRI) Tax on iTax
filing_guides.itax_menu_path: Returns > File Returns > Rental Income Tax
steps: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke using your KRA PIN and password.
Click 'Returns' in the top menu, then select 'File Returns'.
Select 'Rental Income Tax' from the 'Tax Obligation' dropdown.
Select the return period (month and year).
Enter the total gross rent received from all your residential properties during the month.
The system automatically calculates MRI at 7.5% of gross rent.
Note: No deductions or expenses are allowed under the MRI regime. The 7.5% applies to gross rent, not net rent.
Review the calculated tax amount.
Click 'Submit' to file the return.
Download the acknowledgement receipt.
Generate a payment slip and pay the MRI tax by the 20th of the following month.
Pay via M-Pesa (Paybill 572572), bank, or e-payment.
Repeat every month you receive rental income.
documents_needed: KRA PIN and iTax credentials
Records of all rent received during the month (tenant payments)
Lease/tenancy agreements (for reference)
Bank statements showing rental income deposits
Property ownership documents (title deed, sale agreement)
common_mistakes: Trying to deduct expenses (repairs, management fees, mortgage interest) — MRI does not allow any deductions. It is 7.5% of gross rent.
Not being eligible: MRI only applies if annual rental income is between KES 288,000 and KES 15,000,000. Below or above these thresholds, different rules apply.
Including commercial property rent — MRI is for residential properties only. Commercial rent falls under regular income tax.
Not filing in months when no rent was collected — file NIL if you are registered for MRI.
Mixing up MRI with regular income tax on rental income — you must choose one regime.
Not registering for the MRI obligation on iTax before trying to file.
tips: MRI is simpler than regular income tax on rent because there are no deductions to calculate. Just 7.5% of gross rent.
If your rental expenses are high (mortgage, major repairs), regular income tax may be better than MRI — you can deduct expenses there.
Once you opt for MRI, you must stay on it for the full year. You cannot switch mid-year.
Keep records of all tenant payments. M-Pesa and bank transfer records serve as evidence.
If your total annual rent exceeds KES 15M, you must switch to the regular income tax regime.
This is a final tax — rental income filed under MRI is not included in your annual income tax return.

filing_guides.title: How to Pay Presumptive Tax
filing_guides.itax_menu_path: Returns > File Returns > Presumptive Tax (or paid at county level with business permit)
steps: Determine if you qualify: your annual business turnover must be below KES 1,000,000.
Presumptive tax is typically paid when renewing your single business permit at the county government offices.
The county government collects the presumptive tax on behalf of KRA at the time of business permit issuance or renewal.
The amount is a lump sum of KES 15,000 per year (fixed, regardless of actual turnover).
If filing through iTax: Log in to iTax at https://itax.kra.go.ke.
Click 'Returns' > 'File Returns' > select 'Presumptive Tax' from the obligation dropdown.
Enter your business details and the period.
The fixed amount of KES 15,000 will be shown.
Click 'Submit' to file.
Generate a payment slip if paying through iTax rather than at the county office.
Pay via M-Pesa (Paybill 572572), bank, or at the county revenue office.
Keep the payment receipt and your business permit as proof of compliance.
documents_needed: KRA PIN and iTax credentials (if filing online)
Single business permit (or application for one)
National ID or passport
Business registration certificate (if applicable)
Evidence that annual turnover is below KES 1,000,000
common_mistakes: Paying presumptive tax when turnover exceeds KES 1M — you should be on Turnover Tax (TOT) instead.
Not understanding that presumptive tax is a final tax — you do not file a separate income tax return for this business income.
Assuming presumptive tax covers all tax types — it only covers income tax. You may still owe other taxes (VAT if registered, PAYE if you have employees, etc.).
Paying at the county level but not reflecting it on iTax — make sure your KRA records are updated.
Not keeping the receipt — you need proof of payment if KRA queries your compliance.
tips: Presumptive tax is the simplest tax regime in Kenya. KES 15,000 per year, no calculations needed.
If your business is growing and turnover is approaching KES 1M, plan your transition to TOT in advance.
Presumptive tax is ideal for very small businesses: mama mboga, small kiosks, bodaboda operators, etc.
Even though it is paid at the county level, it is a KRA tax. Ensure it reflects in your iTax account.
You can verify your tax status on iTax anytime to confirm the payment was recorded.
If you run multiple businesses, each one under KES 1M may qualify separately — but check with KRA.
